core_engine: power ladder — strike cost, rival pressure, public mastery stakes

chapter_1_beats.beat_text: a ruined coliseum reclaimed by grass, wind moving through the broken tiers, first light. Hana Sumeragi stands at the edge of the ground where a fight they did not choose is already waiting — a figure far stronger across the open space. Their drawn blade trembles, just slightly, at the tip.

The opponent moves first. Hana Sumeragi parries on instinct and is driven back three steps, boots skidding. The shaking in the hands is worse now, and they grip harder to hide it — which only makes it visible.

Hana Sumeragi retreats behind a broken pillar, chest heaving, blade pressed flat to their own forehead. The thought is loud and simple: I cannot do this. The body has already decided to run before the mind admits it.

a retired champion who now sweeps the training hall and watches more than she speaks speaks from the edge of the ground — not a rescue, not a lecture. 'The shaking isn't the enemy. It means you understand exactly how much this costs. Cowards don't shake. They've already left.'

Silence. Hana Sumeragi looks down at their own trembling hand for one long beat. They do not will it to stop. They simply close their fingers around the grip while it is still shaking, and breathe out.

Hana Sumeragi steps out from behind the pillar — straight into the opening, before the fear has finished its sentence. The strike crosses the distance the moment the body would rather flee. Speed lines, the held breath released into the swing.

Blades lock. Hana Sumeragi is still afraid — it is written all over their face — and they are pressing forward anyway, weight driving the guard back inch by inch. Courage is not on the face; it is in the forward foot.

The opponent gives a half-step. Hana Sumeragi lands clean for the first time, and the recoil runs up arms that have stopped shaking — not because the fear is gone, but because there is no longer any room left in the hands for it.

Final image: Hana Sumeragi planted in the center of the ground, blade lowered, breathing hard, the fear still bright in their eyes and the body unmistakably standing IN the fight now. a retired champion who now sweeps the training hall and watches more than she speaks watches from the edge and says nothing. The strike has already answered the question.
